Greenwell and Christine M. {Schubert Kabban}},   journal = {The R Journal},   year = {2014},   volume = {6},   number = {1},   pages = {90--100},   url = {https://doi.org/10.32614/RJ-2014-009},   doi = {10.32614/RJ-2014-009}, }

title: investr — Inverse Estimation in R
text: Inverse estimation, also referred calibration problem, classical well-known problem regression. simple terms, involves use observed value response (specified value mean response) make inference corresponding unknown value explanatory variable. detailed introduction investr published R Journal: “investr: R Package Inverse Estimation”. can track development https://github.com/bgreenwell/investr. report bugs issues, contact main author directly submit https://github.com/bgreenwell/investr/issues. right now, investr supports (univariate) inverse estimation objects class: "lm" - linear models (multiple predictor variables allowed) "glm" - generalized linear models (multiple predictor variables allowed) "nls" - nonlinear least-squares models "lme" - linear mixed-effects models (fit using nlme package) "survfit"/"Surv" - Kaplan-Meier survival curves (fit using survival package), example, median survival time

title: investr — Inverse Estimation in R
text: package also part ChemPhys task view, collection R packages useful analyzing data chemistry physics experiments. packages can installed (including investr) using ctv package (Zeileis, 2005):
code: # Install from CRAN (recommended) install.packages("investr")  # Latest development version, from r-universe install.packages("investr", repos = c("https://bgreenwell.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org")) # Install the ChemPhys task view install.packages("ctv") ctv::install.views("ChemPhys")

title: investr — Inverse Estimation in R
text: binomial regression, estimated lethal dose corresponding specific probability p death often referred LDp. invest obtains estimate LDp inverting fitted mean response link scale. Similarly, confidence interval LDp can obtained inverting confidence interval mean response link scale.  obtain estimate standard error, can use Wald method:
code: # Load required packages library(investr)  # Binomial regression beetle.glm <- glm(cbind(y, n-y) ~ ldose, data = beetle,                    family = binomial(link = "cloglog")) plotFit(beetle.glm, lwd.fit = 2, cex = 1.2, pch = 21, bg = "lightskyblue",          lwd = 2, xlab = "Log dose", ylab = "Probability") # Median lethal dose invest(beetle.glm, y0 = 0.5)    #> estimate    lower    upper  #> 1.778753 1.770211 1.786178  # 90% lethal dose invest(beetle.glm, y0 = 0.9)    #> estimate    lower    upper  #> 1.833221 1.825117 1.843068  # 99% lethal dose invest(beetle.glm, y0 = 0.99)   #> estimate    lower    upper  #> 1.864669 1.853607 1.879133 invest(beetle.glm, y0 = 0.5, interval = "Wald") #>  estimate     lower     upper        se  #> 1.7787530 1.7709004 1.7866057 0.0040065  # The MASS package function dose.p can be used too  MASS::dose.p(beetle.glm, p = 0.5) #>              Dose         SE #> p = 0.5: 1.778753 0.00400654

title: investr — Inverse Estimation in R
text: Multiple predictor variables allowed objects class lm glm. instance, example ?MASS::dose.p can re-created follows:
code: # Load required packages library(MASS)  # Data ldose <- rep(0:5, 2) numdead <- c(1, 4, 9, 13, 18, 20, 0, 2, 6, 10, 12, 16) sex <- factor(rep(c("M", "F"), c(6, 6))) SF <- cbind(numdead, numalive = 20 - numdead) budworm <- data.frame(ldose, numdead, sex, SF)  # Logistic regression budworm.glm <- glm(SF ~ sex + ldose - 1, family = binomial, data = budworm)  # Using dose.p function from package MASS dose.p(budworm.glm, cf = c(1, 3), p = 1/4) #>               Dose        SE #> p = 0.25: 2.231265 0.2499089  # Using invest function from package investr invest(budworm.glm, y0 = 1/4,         interval = "Wald",        x0.name = "ldose",         newdata = data.frame(sex = "F")) #>  estimate     lower     upper        se  #> 2.2312647 1.7414522 2.7210771 0.2499089

title: investr — Inverse Estimation in R
text: data contain actual concentrations agrochemical present soil samples versus weight plant three weeks growth. data stored data frame nasturtium loaded package. simple log-logistic model describes data well:  Three new replicates response (309, 296, 419) unknown concentration interest ($`x_0`$) measured. desired estimate $`x_0`$. intervals rely large sample results normality. practice, bootstrap may reliable:
code: # Log-logistic model for the nasturtium data nas.nls <- nls(weight ~ theta1/(1 + exp(theta2 + theta3 * log(conc))),                start = list(theta1 = 1000, theta2 = -1, theta3 = 1),                data = nasturtium)                 # Plot the fitted model plotFit(nas.nls, lwd.fit = 2) # Inversion method invest(nas.nls, y0 = c(309, 296, 419), interval = "inversion") #> estimate    lower    upper  #> 2.263854 1.772244 2.969355   # Wald method invest(nas.nls, y0 = c(309, 296, 419), interval = "Wald")   #>  estimate     lower     upper        se  #> 2.2638535 1.6888856 2.8388214 0.2847023 # Bootstrap calibration intervals (may take a few seconds) boo <- invest(nas.nls, y0 = c(309, 296, 419), interval = "percentile",               nsim = 9999, seed = 101) boo  # print bootstrap summary #>  estimate     lower     upper        se      bias  #> 2.2638535 1.7889885 2.9380360 0.2946540 0.0281456 plot(boo)  # plot results

title: investr — Inverse Estimation in R

what: Calibration for the simple linear regression model — calibrate
title: Calibration for the simple linear regression model — calibrate
text: function calibrate computes maximum likelihood estimate condfidence interval unknown predictor value corresponds observed value response (vector thereof) specified value mean response. See reference listed details.

title: Calibration for the simple linear regression model — calibrate
code: calibrate(object, ...)  # Default S3 method calibrate(   object,   y0,   interval = c("inversion", "Wald", "none"),   level = 0.95,   mean.response = FALSE,   adjust = c("none", "Bonferroni", "Scheffe"),   k,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'formula' calibrate(formula, data = NULL, ..., subset, na.action = stats::na.fail)  # S3 method for class 'lm' calibrate(   object,   y0,   interval = c("inversion", "Wald", "none"),   level = 0.95,   mean.response = FALSE,   adjust = c("none", "Bonferroni", "Scheffe"),   k,   ... )

title: Calibration for the simple linear regression model — calibrate
text: object matrix, list, data frame, object inherits class stats::lm(). ... Additional optional arguments. present, optional arguments used. y0 value observed response(s) specified value mean response. interval method use forming confidence interval. level numeric scalar 0 1 giving confidence level interval calculated. mean.response Logicial indicating whether confidence intervals correspond observed response(s) (FALSE) specified value mean response (TRUE). Default FALSE. adjust logical value indicating adjustment made critical value used constructing confidence interval. useful calibration curve used k > 0 times. k number times calibration curve used computing confidence interval. needed adjust = TRUE. formula formula form y ~ x. data optional data frame, list environment (object coercible .data.frame data frame) containing variables model. found data, variables taken environment(formula), typically environment stats::lm() called. subset optional vector specifying subset observations used fitting process. na.action function indicates happen data contain NAs.

title: Calibration for the simple linear regression model — calibrate
text: object class "invest" containing following components: estimate estimate x0. lwr lower confidence limit x0. upr upper confidence limit x0. se estimate standard error (Wald interval ). interval method used calculating lower upper (used print method).

title: Calibration for the simple linear regression model — calibrate
text: invest() function general, based numerical techniques find solution. underlying model simple linear regression model normal errors, closed-form expressions exist utilized calibrate function.

title: Calibration for the simple linear regression model — calibrate
text: Graybill, F. ., Iyer, H. K. (1994) Regression analysis: Concepts Applications. Duxbury Press. Miller, R. G. (1981) Simultaneous Statistical Inference. Springer-Verlag.

title: Calibration for the simple linear regression model — calibrate
code: # # Arsenic example (simple linear regression with replication) #  # Inverting a prediction interval for an individual response arsenic.lm <- stats::lm(measured ~ actual, data = arsenic) plotFit(arsenic.lm, interval = "prediction", shade = TRUE,          col.pred = "lightblue") (cal <- calibrate(arsenic.lm, y0 = 3, interval = "inversion")) #> estimate    lower    upper  #> 2.931449 2.536740 3.325140  abline(h = 3) segments(cal$estimate, 3, cal$estimate, par()$usr[3]) arrows(cal$lower, 3, cal$lower, par()$usr[3]) arrows(cal$upper, 3, cal$upper, par()$usr[3])   # # Crystal weight example (simple linear regression) #  # Inverting a confidence interval for the mean response crystal.lm <- stats::lm(weight ~ time, data = crystal) plotFit(crystal.lm, interval = "confidence", shade = TRUE,         col.conf = "lightblue") (cal <- calibrate(crystal.lm, y0 = 8, interval = "inversion",                    mean.response = TRUE)) #> estimate    lower    upper  #> 15.88820 14.65896 17.15963  abline(h = 8) segments(cal$estimate, 8, cal$estimate, par()$usr[3]) arrows(cal$lower, 8, cal$lower, par()$usr[3]) arrows(cal$upper, 8, cal$upper, par()$usr[3])   # Wald interval and approximate standard error based on the delta method calibrate(crystal.lm, y0 = 8, interval = "Wald", mean.response = TRUE) #>   estimate      lower      upper         se  #> 15.8881952 14.6526267 17.1237638  0.5670834

what: Inverse estimation for linear, nonlinear, and generalized linear models — invest
title: Inverse estimation for linear, nonlinear, and generalized linear models — invest
text: Provides point interval estimates unknown predictor value corresponds observed value response (vector thereof) specified value mean response. See references listed details.

title: Inverse estimation for linear, nonlinear, and generalized linear models — invest
code: invest(object, y0, ...)  # S3 method for class 'lm' invest(   object,   y0,   interval = c("inversion", "Wald", "percentile", "none"),   level = 0.95,   mean.response = FALSE,   x0.name,   newdata,   data,   boot.type = c("parametric", "nonparametric"),   nsim = 999,   seed = NULL,   progress = FALSE,   lower,   upper,   extendInt = "no",   tol = .Machine$double.eps^0.25,   maxiter = 1000,   adjust = c("none", "Bonferroni"),   k,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'glm' invest(   object,   y0,   interval = c("inversion", "Wald", "percentile", "none"),   level = 0.95,   lower,   upper,   x0.name,   newdata,   data,   extendInt = "no",   tol = .Machine$double.eps^0.25,   maxiter = 1000,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'nls' invest(   object,   y0,   interval = c("inversion", "Wald", "percentile", "none"),   level = 0.95,   mean.response = FALSE,   data,   boot.type = c("parametric", "nonparametric"),   nsim = 1,   seed = NULL,   progress = FALSE,   lower,   upper,   extendInt = "no",   tol = .Machine$double.eps^0.25,   maxiter = 1000,   adjust = c("none", "Bonferroni"),   k,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'lme' invest(   object,   y0,   interval = c("inversion", "Wald", "percentile", "none"),   level = 0.95,   mean.response = FALSE,   data,   lower,   upper,   q1,   q2,   extendInt = "no",   tol = .Machine$double.eps^0.25,   maxiter = 1000,   ... )

title: Inverse estimation for linear, nonlinear, and generalized linear models — invest
text: object object inherits class stats::lm(), stats::glm(), stats::nls(), nlme::lme(). y0 value observed response(s) specified value mean response. stats::glm() objects, y0 scale response variable (e.g., number 0 1 binomial families). ... Additional optional arguments. present, optional arguments used. interval type interval required. level numeric scalar 0 1 giving confidence level interval calculated. mean.response Logical indicating whether confidence intervals correspond individual response (FALSE) mean response (TRUE). stats::glm() objects, always TRUE. x0.name multiple linear regression, character string giving name predictor variable interest. newdata multiple linear regression, data.frame giving values interest predictor variables (.e., x0.name). data optional data frame. required object$data NULL. boot.type Character string specifying type bootstrap use interval = "percentile". Options "parametric" "nonparametric". nsim Positive integer specifying number bootstrap simulations; bootstrap B (R). seed Optional argument set.seed(). progress Logical indicating whether display text-based progress bar bootstrap simulation. lower lower endpoint interval searched. upper upper endpoint interval searched. extendInt Character string specifying interval c(lower, upper) extended directly produce error inverse prediction function differing signs endpoints. default, "", keeps search interval hence produces error. Can abbreviated. See documentation base R function uniroot details. tol desired accuracy passed stats::uniroot(). Recommend minimum 1e-10. maxiter maximum number iterations passed uniroot. adjust logical value indicating adjustment made critical value used calculating confidence interval.useful calibration curve used multiple, say k, times. k number times calibration curve used computing confidence interval. needed adjust = "Bonferroni". q1 Optional lower cutoff used forming confidence intervals. used object inherits class nlme::lme(). Defaults stats::qnorm((1+level)/2). q2 Optional upper cutoff used forming confidence intervals. used object inherits class nlme::lme(). Defaults stats::qnorm((1-level)/2).

title: Inverse estimation for linear, nonlinear, and generalized linear models — invest
text: Returns object class "invest" , interval = "percentile", class c("invest", "bootCal", "boot"). generic function plot() can used plot output bootstrap simulation interval = "percentile", result also valid "boot" object, works functions boot package (e.g., boot::boot.ci() "norm", "basic", "perc" interval types). object class "invest" containing following components: estimate estimate x0. lwr lower confidence limit x0. upr upper confidence limit x0. se estimate standard error (Wald percentile intervals ). bias bootstrap estimate bias (percentile interval ). bootreps Vector bootstrap replicates (percentile interval ). nsim number bootstrap replicates (percentile interval ). interval method used calculating lower upper (used print() method).

title: Inverse estimation for linear, nonlinear, and generalized linear models — invest
text: Greenwell, B. M. (2014). Topics Statistical Calibration. Ph.D. thesis, Air Force Institute Technology. URL https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/pdfs/ADA598921.pdf Greenwell, B. M., Schubert Kabban, C. M. (2014). investr: R Package Inverse Estimation. R Journal, 6(1), 90–100. URL http://journal.r-project.org/archive/2014-1/greenwell-kabban.pdf. Graybill, F. ., Iyer, H. K. (1994). Regression analysis: Concepts Applications. Duxbury Press. Huet, S., Bouvier, ., Poursat, M-., Jolivet, E.  (2004) Statistical Tools Nonlinear Regression: Practical Guide S-PLUS R Examples. Springer. Norman, D. R., Smith H. (2014). Applied Regression Analysis. John Wiley & Sons. Oman, Samuel D. (1998). Calibration Random Slopes. Biometrics 85(2): 439–449. doi:10.1093/biomet/85.2.439. Seber, G. . F., Wild, C. J. (1989) Nonlinear regression. Wiley.

title: Inverse estimation for linear, nonlinear, and generalized linear models — invest
code: # # Dobson's beetle data (generalized linear model) #  # Complementary log-log model mod <- glm(cbind(y, n-y) ~ ldose, data = beetle,             family = binomial(link = "cloglog")) plotFit(mod, pch = 19, cex = 1.2, lwd = 2,          xlab = "Log dose of carbon disulphide",         interval = "confidence", shade = TRUE,          col.conf = "lightskyblue")   # Approximate 95% confidence intervals and standard error for LD50 invest(mod, y0 = 0.5) #> estimate    lower    upper  #> 1.778753 1.770211 1.786178  invest(mod, y0 = 0.5, interval = "Wald") #>  estimate     lower     upper        se  #> 1.7787530 1.7709004 1.7866057 0.0040065   # # Nasturtium example (nonlinear least-squares with replication) #  # Log-logistic model mod <- nls(weight ~ theta1/(1 + exp(theta2 + theta3 * log(conc))),            start = list(theta1 = 1000, theta2 = -1, theta3 = 1),            data = nasturtium) plotFit(mod, lwd.fit = 2)              # Compute approximate 95% calibration intervals invest(mod, y0 = c(309, 296, 419), interval = "inversion") #> estimate    lower    upper  #> 2.263854 1.772244 2.969355  invest(mod, y0 = c(309, 296, 419), interval = "Wald")   #>  estimate     lower     upper        se  #> 2.2638535 1.6888856 2.8388214 0.2847023   # Bootstrap calibration intervals. In general, nsim should be as large as  # reasonably possible (say, nsim = 9999). boo <- invest(mod, y0 = c(309, 296, 419), interval = "percentile",                nsim = 300, seed = 101) boo  # print bootstrap summary #>  estimate     lower     upper        se      bias  #> 2.2638535 1.7387952 2.8979535 0.3006779 0.0126353  plot(boo)  # plot results   # # Bladder volume example (random coefficient model) #  # Load required packages library(nlme)  # Plot data plot(HD^(3/2) ~ volume, data = bladder, pch = 19,        col = adjustcolor("black", alpha.f = 0.5))          # Fit a random intercept and slope model bladder <- na.omit(bladder) ris <- lme(HD^(3/2) ~ volume, data = bladder, random = ~volume|subject) invest(ris, y0 = 500) #>  estimate     lower     upper  #>  8.013671  4.097209 12.046123  invest(ris, y0 = 500, interval = "Wald") #>  estimate     lower     upper        se  #>  8.013671  4.053726 11.973616  2.020417

what: Inverse estimation for Kaplan-Meier survival curves — invest.survfit
title: Inverse estimation for Kaplan-Meier survival curves — invest.survfit
text: Inverts Kaplan-Meier estimate survival function: given survival probability y0, methods estimate survival time \(t\) \(S(t) = y_0\), along confidence interval obtained inverting pointwise confidence band survival curve (inversion idea used invest() regression models). example, default y0 = 0.5 gives median survival time.

title: Inverse estimation for Kaplan-Meier survival curves — invest.survfit
code: # S3 method for class 'survfit' invest(object, y0 = 0.5, level = object$conf.int, ...)  # S3 method for class 'Surv' invest(object, y0 = 0.5, level = 0.95, ...)

title: Inverse estimation for Kaplan-Meier survival curves — invest.survfit
text: object object inherits class "survfit" (fitted Kaplan-Meier curve survival::survfit()) class "Surv" (censored response object survival::Surv(), Kaplan-Meier curve fit internally). y0 numeric scalar (0, 1) giving survival probability invert curve. Default 0.5 (.e., median survival time). level numeric scalar 0 1 giving confidence level interval. "survfit" objects, must match conf.int level curve fit (default); refit survival::survfit(..., conf.int = level) different level. "Surv" objects, internal fit uses level directly. ... Additional optional arguments. present, optional arguments used.

title: Inverse estimation for Kaplan-Meier survival curves — invest.survfit
text: object class "invest" containing components estimate, lower, upper, interval (always "inversion"). Components NA whenever survival curve (confidence limit) never drops y0; particular, upper frequently NA small samples, meaning upper confidence limit indeterminate (infinite).

title: Inverse estimation for Kaplan-Meier survival curves — invest.survfit
text: methods support single survival curve; "survfit" object multiple strata, invert stratum's curve separately (e.g., via survfit[] subscripting).

title: Inverse estimation for Kaplan-Meier survival curves — invest.survfit
code: if (requireNamespace("survival", quietly = TRUE)) {    # Median survival time (with 95% confidence limits) for the acute   # myelogenous leukemia data   km <- survival::survfit(survival::Surv(time, status) ~ 1,                           data = survival::aml)   invest(km)  # y0 = 0.5 is the median    # Same, but fitting the curve internally from a censored response   invest(survival::Surv(survival::aml$time, survival::aml$status))    # Time at which 75% survival is reached   invest(km, y0 = 0.75)  } #> estimate    lower    upper  #>       12        8       30

title: Plotting fitted models — plotFit
text: Generic function plotting predictions various types fitted models. plotFit currently supports objects class stats::lm(), stats::glm(), stats::nls(). default method also exists may used plotting fitted mean response model fits (e.g., MASS::lqs() MASS::rlm() MASS package).

title: Plotting fitted models — plotFit
code: plotFit(object, ...)  # Default S3 method plotFit(   object,   type = c("response", "link"),   interval = c("none", "both", "confidence", "prediction"),   level = 0.95,   data,   adjust = c("none", "Bonferroni", "Scheffe"),   k,   ...,   shade = FALSE,   extend.range = FALSE,   hide = TRUE,   col.conf = if (shade) grDevices::grey(0.7) else "black",   col.pred = if (shade) grDevices::grey(0.9) else "black",   border.conf = col.conf,   border.pred = col.pred,   col.fit = "black",   lty.conf = if (shade) 1 else 2,   lty.pred = if (shade) 1 else 3,   lty.fit = 1,   lwd.conf = 1,   lwd.pred = 1,   lwd.fit = 1,   n = 500,   xlab,   ylab,   xlim,   ylim )

title: Plotting fitted models — plotFit
text: object fitted model object. Typically, object inherits class stats::lm(), stats::glm(), stats::nls() (others may work ). ... Additional optional arguments passed plot(). type type prediction required. default scale response variable; alternative "link" scale linear predictor. option used plotting stats::glm() objects. interval character string indicating prediction band, confidence band, , none plotted. level desired confidence level. data optional data frame containing variables model. adjust character string indicating type adjustment () make confidence/prediction bands. k integer used computing critical value confidence/prediction bands. needed adjust = "Bonferroni", adjust = "Scheffe" interval = "prediction". shade logical value indicating band shaded. extend.range logical value indicating fitted regression line bands () extend edges plot. Default FALSE. hide logical value indicating fitted model plotted top points (FALSE) behind (TRUE). Default TRUE. col.conf Shade color confidence band. col.pred Shade color prediction band. border.conf color use confidence band border. border.pred color use prediction band border. col.fit color use fitted line. lty.conf Line type use confidence band border. lty.pred Line type use prediction band border. lty.fit Line type use fitted regression line. lwd.conf Line width use confidence band border. lwd.pred Line width use prediction band border. lwd.fit Line width use fitted regression line. n number predictor values evaluate fitted model (larger gives smoother plot). xlab title x axis. ylab title y axis. xlim x limits (x1, x2) plot. ylim y limits (y1, y2) plot.

title: Plotting fitted models — plotFit
text: return value (called side effects).

title: Plotting fitted models — plotFit
text: default, plotted intervals unadjusted (.e., pointwise) intervals. simultaneous intervals, use adjust = "Bonferroni" adjust = "Scheffe". Bonferroni adjustment, must specify value k, number intervals coverage hold simultaneously. Scheffe adjustment, specifying value k required interval = "prediction"; interval = "confidence", k set equal \(p\), number regression parameters. example, object simple linear regression model, calling plotFit interval = "confidence" adjust = "Scheffe" plot Working-Hotelling band. Confidence/prediction bands nonlinear regression (.e., objects class stats::nls()) based linear approximation described Bates & Watts (2007).

title: Plotting fitted models — plotFit
text: Bates, D. M., Watts, D. G. (2007) Nonlinear Regression Analysis Applications. Wiley. Florent Baty, Christian Ritz, Sandrine Charles, Martin Brutsche, Jean-Pierre Flandrois, Marie-Laure Delignette-Muller (2015). Toolbox Nonlinear Regression R: Package nlstools. Journal Statistical Software, 66(5), 1-21.

title: Plotting fitted models — plotFit
code: # A nonlinear least squares example (see ?datasets::Puromycin and  # ?investr::predFit) data(Puromycin, package = "datasets") Puromycin2 <- Puromycin[Puromycin$state == "treated", ][, 1:2] Puro.nls <- nls(rate ~ Vm * conc/(K + conc), data = Puromycin2,                 start = c(Vm = 200, K = 0.05)) plotFit(Puro.nls, interval = "both", pch = 19, shade = TRUE,          col.conf = "skyblue4", col.pred = "lightskyblue2")   #> Error in eval(stats::getCall(object)$data): object 'Puromycin2' not found

what: Predictions from a Fitted Model — predFit
title: Predictions from a Fitted Model — predFit
text: Generic prediction method various types fitted models. predFit can used obtain standard errors fitted values adjusted/unadjusted confidence/prediction intervals objects class "lm", "nls", "glm".

title: Predictions from a Fitted Model — predFit
code: predFit(object, ...)  # Default S3 method predFit(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'lm' predFit(   object,   newdata,   se.fit = FALSE,   interval = c("none", "confidence", "prediction"),   level = 0.95,   adjust = c("none", "Bonferroni", "Scheffe"),   k,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'glm' predFit(   object,   newdata,   type = c("link", "response"),   se.fit = FALSE,   interval = c("none", "confidence"),   level = 0.95,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'nls' predFit(   object,   newdata,   se.fit = FALSE,   interval = c("none", "confidence", "prediction"),   level = 0.95,   adjust = c("none", "Bonferroni", "Scheffe"),   k,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'lme' predFit(object, newdata, se.fit = FALSE, ...)

title: Predictions from a Fitted Model — predFit
text: object object inherits class "lm", "glm", "nls", "lme". ... Additional optional arguments. present, optional arguments used. newdata optional data frame look variables predict. omitted, fitted values used. se.fit logical vaue indicating standard errors required. Default FALSE. interval Type interval calculated. Can one "none" (default), "confidence", "prediction". Default "none". level numeric scalar 0 1 giving confidence level intervals () calculated. Default 0.95. adjust logical value indicating adjustment made critical value used calculating confidence interval. useful calibration curve used multiple, say k, times. Default FALSE. k number times calibration curve used computing confidence/prediction interval. needed adjust = "Bonferroni". type Character string specifying type prediction. Current options type = "link" (default) type = "response".

title: Predictions from a Fitted Model — predFit
text: se.fit = FALSE, predFit() returns vector predictions matrix predictions bounds column names fit, lwr, upr interval "none". (function meant internal use.) se.fit = TRUE, list following components returned: fit vector matrix described ; se.fit vector containing standard errors predicted means; residual.scale residual standard deviations; df residual degrees freedom.

title: Predictions from a Fitted Model — predFit
text: Confidence prediction intervals linear models (.e., "lm" objects) obtained according usual formulas. Nonlinear generalized linear models (.e., "nls" "glm" objects), hand, rely Taylor-series approximations standard errors used forming intervals. Approximate standard errors fitted values linear mixed-effects models (.e., "lme" objects) can also computed; however, rely approximate variance-covariance matrix fixed-effects estimates often estimate true standard error. accurate standard errors can obtained using parametric bootstrap; see bootMer function lme4 package details. linear nonlinear models, possible request adjusted confidence prediction intervals using Bonferroni method (adjust = "Bonferroni") Scheffe's method (adjust = "Scheffe"). Bonferroni adjustment, must specify value k, number intervals coverage hold simultaneously. Scheffe adjustment, specifying value k required interval = "prediction"; interval = "confidence", k set equal \(p\), number regression parameters. example, calling plotFit "lm" objects interval = "confidence" adjust = "Scheffe" plot Working-Hotelling band.

title: Predictions from a Fitted Model — predFit

title: investr (development version)
text: New invest() methods "survfit" "Surv" objects (survival package) estimate survival time Kaplan-Meier curve reaches given survival probability (e.g., median survival time), confidence limits obtained inverting curve’s pointwise confidence band (#30). invest() results interval = "percentile" now also inherit class "boot", work directly functions boot package boot::boot.ci() (#32). nlme moved Imports Suggests; "lme"-specific code paths now check requireNamespace() (#47). Fixed calibrate.lm() silently mishandling models poly()-transformed predictors; now reads predictor model frame errors informatively instead (#48). Fixed formula g inversion-interval vignette, broken regression prior “typo fix” (#51). Fixed predFit() se.fit = TRUE failing "nls" models one predictor variable (#53). Fixed invest() predFit() failing fitted model created inside function, data argument ; training/prediction data now reconstructed via model.frame() environment captured model’s formula, instead evaluating object$call$data caller’s frame (#41, #42, #45). invest() now validates newdata columns classes (, factors, levels) compatible fitted model’s training data, rather failing confusing downstream error silently returning nonsensical result (#36). “Use plotFit guidance” hint failed confidence-limit search longer appears multi-predictor lm/glm models, since plotFit() doesn’t support one predictor variable (#35).

title: investr 1.4.2
text: CRAN release: 2022-03-31 Fixed output predFit() situations whenever standard errors confidence/predictions bands requested. New data sets bladder (repeated measures data set) whisky. Fixed typos documentation throughout package. Updated URLs throughout package. Better y-axis limits x-axis log scale (e.g., using log = “x”). Fixes issue #27. Better default y-axis label using plotFit() model transformed response. example, calling plotFit(fit) fit <- lm(sqrt(dist) ~ speed, data = cars) default y-axis label "sqrt(dist)". plotFit() completely re-written using much less code. predFit() (hence plotFit()) now works "nls" objects fit using Golub-Pereyra algorithm (.e., algorithm = "plinear"); however, confidence/prediction bands still available. New introductory vignette.

title: investr 1.4.0
text: CRAN release: 2016-04-09 Multiple predictor variables allowed "lm" "glm" objects. non-base package functions now imported. generic function predFit() now exported. function used investr obtain predictions, hence, inverse predictions. example, predFit() can used obtain prediction intervals nonlinear least-squares fits (.e., models class "nls"). Improved tests test coverage. plotFit() gained methods "rlm" "lqs" objects package MASS.

title: investr 1.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2015-03-25 invest() now accepts objects class "glm" (experimental). Functions calibrate() invest() now return object class "invest".

title: investr 1.2.1
text: CRAN release: 2015-01-06 Cleaned documentation. Added AnyNA() function using older versions R.

title: investr 1.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2014-12-23 Cleaned examples. Added bootstrap option invest().

title: investr 1.1.2
text: CRAN release: 2014-09-15 Changed tests satisfy CRAN check.

title: investr 1.1.1
text: CRAN release: 2014-09-09 Updated citation file. Minor code changes. plotFit() now plot models transformed responses correctly. Fixed error causing invest() fail missing data argument. Added tests.

title: investr 1.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2014-07-14 invest() now accepts objects class "lme" (experimental). minor bug fixes code improvements. Added tests.

title: investr 1.0.1
text: CRAN release: 2014-04-19 minor bug fixes. Slightly better documentation.

title: investr 1.0
text: CRAN release: 2013-08-09 Initial release.
